snippet.title: Mastercard Holding a Market Cap of Over $350 Billion
snippet.description:
Day 15 of 100 Days, 100 Stories 1️⃣5️⃣

The force behind it? Well, let us introduce you to Michael Miebach, the CEO of Mastercard. 🤝💼

He is a leader who has devoted more than 20 years of his professional career to the company and made sure that it skyrockets as it does today. 🚀🌍

Michael is one of the reasons why every person now carries a Mastercard and has opted for the digital economy. 💻📈

Takeaways from Michael’s journey:

- Consistency is the key. If you decide to give up midway, you will never be able to see the end of the tunnel. 🗝️🚀

- Be informed. Serving a market so big, it is important that you are always aware of what’s new to be at the top of your game. 📊🌟

snippet.title: The Game-Changer: How Michael Miebach is Revolutionizing the Financial Services Industry
snippet.description:
Welcome to Mindful Thoughts! in this video on Michael Miebach, the CEO of Mastercard. We will take a look at Miebach's career history and mindset, to understand how he became a successful and influential business leader.

Michael Miebach was born in Germany and holds a degree in economics from the University of Bonn. He began his career in banking at Deutsche Bank, where he worked in various roles for almost a decade. In 2001, Miebach moved to Citigroup, where he spent nine years in leadership positions in the bank's credit card business in Europe, the Middle East, and Africa. During his time at Citigroup, Miebach helped launch several new credit card products and led the bank's acquisition of the Egg credit card business in the UK.

In 2010, Miebach joined Mastercard as Division President for the company's Middle East and Africa region. He subsequently held leadership roles in other regions, including serving as President of Mastercard's North American markets and as Chief Product Officer. In March 2020, Miebach was appointed President of Mastercard, and he became the company's CEO in January 2021, succeeding Ajay Banga.

snippet.title: The Multimillionaire Business of Mastercard (Documentary)
snippet.description:
Discover the Strange Method that Mastercard used to became the King of Credit Cards and turned the company into leader of the financial industry and make them bigger than visa and amex.

⭐️ Subscribe to Wealth Footprints and discover the untold truth and mysteries of wealth, money, business, entrepreneur, corporations and global economic conspiracy. 

How MasterCard Transformed into the Global Financial Powerhouse That Empowers You! Discover the riveting journey of MasterCard, the trailblazer that reshaped the world of payments. From its humble beginnings to becoming the largest financial company on the planet, this captivating video takes you behind the scenes of MasterCard's incredible rise to dominance. Uncover the strategies, innovations, and unwavering commitment that propelled MasterCard to the forefront of the industry, revolutionizing the way you transact and empowering you to embrace a world of endless possibilities. Get inspired by the untold story of MasterCard's extraordinary transformation!

MasterCard: Pioneering the Transformation of Global Payments

In today's interconnected and digital world, money is the lifeblood that fuels our aspirations, dreams, and desires. It empowers us to unlock new opportunities, indulge in unforgettable experiences, and make our mark on the world. However, there are some things that money alone cannot buy. It cannot buy love, happiness, or the intangible moments that create lasting memories. But for everything else, there's MasterCard – a beacon of trust, innovation, and financial empowerment.

MasterCard, with its iconic slogan, has transcended the realm of mere payment cards to become a global brand that resonates with millions around the world. As the second largest card network in the United States, MasterCard boasts an impressive market share, accounting for over a quarter of all purchase volumes made using a payment card. Its international dominance is equally staggering, with nearly a quarter of the $624 billion spent globally via payment cards in 2022 being made using a MasterCard.

The reach of MasterCard is unparalleled, making it the most accepted card in terms of the number of countries. Regardless of location, you'll likely find a merchant who welcomes the familiar red and yellow logo. This ubiquity is a testament to the trust and reliability that MasterCard has established over the years, making it an indispensable companion for individuals, businesses, and economies alike.

snippet.title: Mastercard's Chief Says Blockchain Can Bring Much More Value to Financial Services
snippet.description:
Payment giant Mastercard finds that crypto and blockchain are useful and can bring much more value to financial industries as a whole, but only when they tackle the security and ease of use issues in order to build trust. 

Raj Dhamodharan, Mastercard’s EVP and head of crypto and blockchain, spoke during a North America Innovation Day event, and said that the novel technologies of blockchain and crypto are becoming more popular globally.

However, Dhamodharan noted, the lack of security and complex user interactions limit the technologies' use cases and utilities.

Therefore, while crypto technology has the ability to store and move capital and value, he argued, these use cases become sparse when safety and simplicity are not priorities.

The EVP was quoted by TechCrunch as saying that, 

"Instilling trust in the blockchain ecosystem is a critical step in realizing its full potential."

Similarly, Johan Gerber, EVP of Security and Cyber Innovation, said that,

"What you need for this tech to scale globally is interoperability and underlying security of trust."

When these key points are accomplished, Dhamodharan opined, it will be a lot easier for various companies, both traditional and new players, to enter the Web3 industry and engage with it. 

There is capital and talent coming into the industry, and there is plenty of innovation to be seen, but the technology holds more potential, Dhamodharan said. 

"People look at crypto and think of it as an investment, but there’s a whole sector that’s a lot more useful for financial industries as a whole. The technology itself holds a lot of promise."

Gerber added that Mastercard "aims to provide a technological foundation that allows everyone from small startups to massive financial institutions to innovate and build upon."

Not a Newcomer.

Mastercard is not new to the industry. Several developments have been announced over the past few months alone.

As reported in October last year, the company debuted a new piece of software, Crypto Secure, powered by CipherTrace, meant to help banks identify and cut off transactions from fraud-prone crypto exchanges. 

That same month, Dhamodharan said that "someday soon" we would be able to spend cryptocurrencies as seamlessly as contactless card payments, but that it would require developments in some key areas, including crypto cards, service providers, and easier crypto-to-fiat conversion for payments. 

In November, CEO Michael Miebach argued that it is "entirely possible," that one day everybody will be paying with Bitcoin (BTC), "but I think it’s a long way to go before crypto becomes mainstream."

Also in November, Mastercard launched Start Path Crypto, a global engagement program to help blockchain, crypto, and digital asset startups scale.

Meanwhile, in June 2022, the company said it would begin enabling non-fungible token (NFT) trading for a range of crypto-ecosystem platforms, including marketplaces, metaverse gaming titles, and Web3 infrastructure providers.

And in February this year, Satvik Sethi, the former NFT Product Lead at Mastercard, made a unique announcement: he surprised the NFT community by minting his resignation letter as an open-edition NFT on Ethereum via the digital collectibles protocol Manifold. 

____

snippet.title: B20 Summit Live: AI Is Transforming Economies | AI Opportunities, Regulations
snippet.description:
Artificial Intelligence (AI) is one of the biggest technological breakthroughs of the 21st century and touches upon almost every sector of the Indian economy. The rise in digitalization and globalization has opened new avenues for AI to reshape economies in a transformational and innovative way. Technology is altering the world and is impacting society, economies and governance as never before. Hence, countries and businesses are fast waking up to utilize the potential of AI which is finding an application in diverse sectors such as retail, finance, manufacturing, healthcare, IT and e-commerce, agriculture, waste management, etc. However, AI's proliferation also warrants thoughtful regulations to address ethical concerns, data privacy, and algorithmic biases. This session will discuss and deliberate on how to reap the opportunities and address the challenges that AI has brought for Business, Talent, Societies and Environment.
On the panel: 
Brad Smith, President and Vice Chairman, Microsoft, US
N Chandrasekaran, Chair, B20 India and Executive Chairman, Tata Sons, India
Michael Miebach, Chief Executive Officer, Mastercard, USA
Arvind Krishna, Chairman and Chief Executive Officer, IBM, USA
In conversation with: Shantanu Narayen, Chair and Chief Executive Officer, Adobe, USA

snippet.title: The Outcomes Over Hours Show with Ari Sarker, Mastercard
snippet.description:
“I have carved out a sacrosanct morning routine that sets me up for my day – it starts with not checking my phone at all for at least the first 90 mins after waking up”

Recently on the Outcomes Over Hours Show, I had the pleasure of chatting with @Ari Sarker, President of MasterCard Asia Pacific.

Ari’s leadership insights are some of the best I’ve heard regarding how to effectively lead in a hybrid world.  

We cover the importance of creating time and space for yourself (Ari doesn’t check his phone in his first hour upon waking), advice for how to build your career and why being clear on your purpose is a critical component for a successful career.

How do you create time for yourself in your day, so you can focus on what really matters?

#outcomesoverhours #leadership #hybridwork #purpose

snippet.title: "Meet Ajay Banga, the new leader of World Bank"
snippet.description:
Ajay Banga is an Indian-American business executive who served as the CEO of Mastercard from 2010 to 2021.

He was Awarded the Padma Shri in 2016.

He was born on April 17, 1960, in Pune, India, and grew up in Hyderabad.

Banga received a Bachelor of Arts degree in Economics from Delhi University in 1981 and an MBA from the Indian Institute of Management, Ahmedabad in 1984.

After graduation, he worked for Nestle in India before moving to the United States in 1986 to work for PepsiCo.

In 1996, Banga joined Citigroup, where he held various positions over the next 13 years, including CEO of Citigroup's International Global Consumer Group.

In 2009, he joined Mastercard as the company's President and Chief Operating Officer, and was later named CEO in 2010.

Banga retired as CEO of Mastercard in 2021 and was succeeded by Michael Miebach.

He currently serves on several corporate and non-profit boards, including the Council on Foreign Relations, the International Chamber of Commerce, and the United States-India Strategic Partnership Forum.

अजय बंगा एक भारतीय-अमेरिकी व्यवसाय कार्यकारी हैं, जिन्होंने 2010 से 2021 तक मास्टरकार्ड के सीईओ के रूप में कार्य किया।

उन्हें 2016 में पद्म श्री से सम्मानित किया गया था।

उनका जन्म 17 अप्रैल, 1960 को पुणे, भारत में हुआ था और वे हैदराबाद में पले-बढ़े।

बंगा ने 1981 में दिल्ली विश्वविद्यालय से अर्थशास्त्र में कला स्नातक की डिग्री प्राप्त की और 1984 में भारतीय प्रबंधन संस्थान, अहमदाबाद से एमबीए किया।

स्नातक स्तर की पढ़ाई के बाद, उन्होंने पेप्सिको के लिए काम करने के लिए 1986 में संयुक्त राज्य अमेरिका जाने से पहले भारत में नेस्ले के लिए काम किया।

1996 में, बंगा सिटीग्रुप में शामिल हो गए, जहां उन्होंने अगले 13 वर्षों में विभिन्न पदों पर कार्य किया, जिसमें सिटीग्रुप के इंटरनेशनल ग्लोबल कंज्यूमर ग्रुप के सीईओ भी शामिल थे।

2009 में, वह कंपनी के अध्यक्ष और मुख्य परिचालन अधिकारी के रूप में मास्टरकार्ड में शामिल हुए, और बाद में 2010 में उन्हें सीईओ नामित किया गया।

बंगा 2021 में मास्टरकार्ड के सीईओ के रूप में सेवानिवृत्त हुए और उनकी जगह माइकल माइबैक ने ली।

वह वर्तमान में काउंसिल ऑन फॉरेन रिलेशंस, इंटरनेशनल चैंबर ऑफ कॉमर्स और यूनाइटेड स्टेट्स-इंडिया स्ट्रेटेजिक पार्टनरशिप फोरम सहित कई कॉर्पोरेट और गैर-लाभकारी बोर्डों में कार्य करता है।

He currently serves on several corporate and non-profit boards, including the Council on Foreign Relations, the International Chamber of Commerce, and the United States-India Strategic Partnership Forum.

अजय बंगा एक भारतीय-अमेरिकी व्यवसाय कार्यकारी हैं, जिन्होंने 2010 से 2021 तक मास्टरकार्ड के सीईओ के रूप में कार्य किया।

उन्हें 2016 में पद्म श्री से सम्मानित किया गया था।

उनका जन्म 17 अप्रैल, 1960 को पुणे, भारत में हुआ था और वे हैदराबाद में पले-बढ़े।

बंगा ने 1981 में दिल्ली विश्वविद्यालय से अर्थशास्त्र में कला स्नातक की डिग्री प्राप्त की और 1984 में भारतीय प्रबंधन संस्थान, अहमदाबाद से एमबीए किया।

स्नातक स्तर की पढ़ाई के बाद, उन्होंने पेप्सिको के लिए काम करने के लिए 1986 में संयुक्त राज्य अमेरिका जाने से पहले भारत में नेस्ले के लिए काम किया।

1996 में, बंगा सिटीग्रुप में शामिल हो गए, जहां उन्होंने अगले 13 वर्षों में विभिन्न पदों पर कार्य किया, जिसमें सिटीग्रुप के इंटरनेशनल ग्लोबल कंज्यूमर ग्रुप के सीईओ भी शामिल थे।

2009 में, वह कंपनी के अध्यक्ष और मुख्य परिचालन अधिकारी के रूप में मास्टरकार्ड में शामिल हुए, और बाद में 2010 में उन्हें सीईओ नामित किया गया।

बंगा 2021 में मास्टरकार्ड के सीईओ के रूप में सेवानिवृत्त हुए और उनकी जगह माइकल माइबैक ने ली।

वह वर्तमान में काउंसिल ऑन फॉरेन रिलेशंस, इंटरनेशनल चैंबर ऑफ कॉमर्स और यूनाइटेड स्टेट्स-इंडिया स्ट्रेटेजिक पार्टनरशिप फोरम सहित कई कॉर्पोरेट और गैर-लाभकारी बोर्डों में कार्य करता है।

snippet.title: You Can Make a Difference in Your Community | Noa Tishby in Australia
snippet.description:
People often ask, how can we make a difference in the Jewish community? How can we speak up against hate and antisemitism? You don't need to be a social media activist to make a difference-- it's more simple than you think. Invite your non-Jewish friends out, engage with your coworkers. People cannot hate Jews when they know one. That is the first, small step to making a difference.

Noa Tishby is interviewed before the High Holidays in Australia.
